bytes.topic: Firewalls & NAT — iptables/nftables, connection tracking, SNAT/DNAT
bytes.title: quic — Connection Tracking with NAT and Firewall Rules
bytes.body: When using quic with iptables or nftables, connection tracking is crucial for proper NAT and firewall functionality. quic's connection IDs are used to identify and track connections, allowing for stateful packet inspection. To enable connection tracking with SNAT (Source NAT) or DNAT (Destination NAT), you must specify the connection ID in the firewall rules. The `ctstate` target is used to track connection states. For example, when using iptables, you can use the `conntrack` target to enable connection tracking: `iptables -t mangle -A OUTPUT -p tcp --ctstate NEW -j CONNMARK --ctdir ORIGINAL_DST`. Similarly, with nftables, you can use the `ct` family to create a connection tracking table.
bytes.code_snippet: iptables -t mangle -A OUTPUT -p tcp --ctstate NEW -j CONNMARK --ctdir ORIGINAL_DST

bytes.topic: HTTP/1.1 vs HTTP/2 vs HTTP/3 — multiplexing, head-of-line blocking, QUIC
bytes.title: QUIC: When HTTP/3 Beats HTTP/2 in Multiplexing
bytes.body: When QUIC's connection multiplexing is enabled, HTTP/3 outperforms HTTP/2 due to its reduced head-of-line blocking. QUIC's concurrent stream creation allows multiple streams to transmit data simultaneously, leveraging the available network bandwidth. This is achieved through the quic.ConnectionMultiplexing API, which enables concurrent stream establishment and transmission. As a result, HTTP/3's multiplexing capabilities surpass those of HTTP/2, making it a more reliable choice for high-bandwidth applications.

bytes.topic: TLS over TCP — handshake, session resumption, 0-RTT trade-offs
bytes.title: QuIC TLS Handshake: Session Resumption and 0-RTT Trade-Offs
bytes.body: When a QuIC client initiates a connection, it performs a TLS handshake using the QUIC_TLS_HANDSHAKE frame. The client and server exchange cryptographic parameters, and the server presents its certificate. The client verifies the server's identity and establishes a secure connection. For subsequent connections, the client can use session resumption to quickly establish a secure connection. However, session resumption requires the server to store the session IDs, which can increase memory usage. 0-RTT (zero round-trip time) handshake allows the client to send encrypted data before the handshake is complete, but it requires the client to have a valid session ticket from a previous connection. The trade-off is that 0-RTT handshake reduces the latency but increases the risk of replay attacks.

bytes.topic: Congestion Control — CUBIC, BBR, QUIC's approach, buffer bloat
bytes.title: QUIC's Congestion Control: CUBIC, BBR, and the role of ACKs
bytes.body: QUIC employs a congestion control mechanism based on TCP's CUBIC and BBR algorithms. When a QUIC receiver acknowledges packets, it updates the sender's congestion window. CUBIC uses an exponential backoff algorithm to minimize packet losses during congestion episodes. In contrast, BBR (Bottleneck Bandwidth and Round-trip time) estimates the available bandwidth and adjusts the congestion window accordingly. Unlike TCP, QUIC's congestion control does not rely on packet losses to adjust the window size; instead, it uses ACK packets to monitor the available bandwidth and adjust the congestion window proactively.

bytes.topic: Latency vs Throughput — trade-offs, tuning kernel network stack
bytes.title: Quic: Tuning Latency vs Throughput with MaxUplinkBandwidth
bytes.body: When optimizing a Quic connection, balancing latency and throughput is crucial. The `MaxUplinkBandwidth` parameter controls how aggressively the sender transmits data. Set it too high, and you risk increasing latency due to packet reordering and timeouts. Set it too low, and you may not utilize the available bandwidth. The optimal value depends on the underlying network conditions. To experiment with different values, use the `quic_transport_params_set_max_uplink_bandwidth()` function.
